front: Ruler: Georgian-born dictator who led the Soviet Union through the 1930s and World War II
Ruler: succeeded Vladimir Lenin and ruled the Soviet Union until his death in 1953
Ruler: as a youth in Georgia, wrote Georgian-language poetry (including "To the Moon") under the pen name "Soselo"
Ruler: as a youth, admired and memorized parts of Shota Rustaveli's The Knight in the Panther's Skin, Georgia's national epic
Ruler: historians dispute whether he personally participated in or merely watched the Tiflis bank robbery, which he had planned
Ruler: agitated as a radical in the oil city of Baku, later gaining military fame at Tsaritsyn
Ruler: with Nikolai Bukharin's help, adopted the policy of "Socialism in One Country" over spreading world revolution
Event: caused by Stalin's collectivization and dekulakization policies, exacerbated by the "Law of Spikelets"; Walter Duranty publicly disputed Gareth Jones's reporting on it
Ruler: wrote the article "Dizzy with Success" to blame "excesses" for the famine his own collectivization campaign had caused
Ruler: deported kulaks to labor camps, worsening the famine, before reversing course after Operation Barbarossa
Ruler: had members of the census bureau TsUNKhu arrested after a census revealed a population decrease
Ruler: his kolkhoz collective-farm system, built through Five-Year Plans, caused a famine that killed a fourth of Kazakhstan's population
Event: this Central Asian anti-veiling campaign, under the slogan "To the Attack!," saw public burnings of paranji robes and sparked the Chust Affair protest
Ruler: arrested reformist Jadidi writers such as Abdurauf Fitrat, ending the Jadidi movement in Central Asia
Ruler: forcibly transported hundreds of thousands of ethnic Koreans and Tatars via cattle cars to Muslim-majority Central Asia
Ruler: executed the leaders of the Uzbek Soviet Socialist Republic during his 1930s purges
Ruler: closed churches to advance state atheism under his Second Five-Year Plan, before reversing course during the Great Patriotic War
Event: this late-1930s program, sparked by the assassination of Sergei Kirov, included the show trials of Kamenev and Zinoviev (the Trial of the Sixteen)
Ruler: purged his former ally Nikolai Bukharin, with whom he had earlier collaborated on "Socialism in One Country"
Ruler: was accused by Leon Trotsky of exploiting antisemitism during the Great Purge
Ruler: appointed Nikolai Yezhov to head the NKVD ahead of the Great Purge
Ruler: later appointed Lavrentiy Beria to lead the secret-police NKVD, following the model of Lenin's Cheka
Ruler: executed military theorist Mikhail Tukhachevsky in a purge of the Red Army's generals, weakening the army before Hitler's invasion
Event: this trial and other accusations of "wrecking" (sabotage), later echoed in the Metropolitan-Vickers Trial, were part of the Great Purge
Ruler: sent Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn to a gulag for criticizing him
Ruler: Katyusha rocket-launcher designer Georgy Langemak was executed during his Great Purge
Ruler: had lackeys doctor official photographs to erase purged enemies like Lev Kamenev and Grigory Zinoviev
Ruler: signed the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact with Nazi Germany, splitting Poland in half, before Hitler broke it with Operation Barbarossa
work: World War II: Barbarossa & the Eastern Front

front: War: this German invasion broke the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact, catching the Red Army weakened by Stalin's prewar purges
work: World War II: Barbarossa & the Eastern Front

front: Event: perpetrated by the NKVD under Lavrentiy Beria against Polish officers and intelligentsia, later blamed on the Nazis in Soviet textbooks
work: World War II: Barbarossa & the Eastern Front

front: Ruler: met with Churchill and Roosevelt as one of the "Big Three" at the Yalta and Tehran Conferences
work: World War II: Barbarossa & the Eastern Front

front: Ruler: Operation Bagration is called the "fifth of the ten blows" attributed to him in Soviet accounts of the war
work: World War II: Barbarossa & the Eastern Front

front: Ruler: picked Vyacheslav Molotov to lead the Soviet atomic bomb project
work: World War II: Barbarossa & the Eastern Front

front: Ruler: relied on general Georgy Zhukov, hero of Khalkhin Gol and the occupation of Bessarabia, through the rest of the war
work: World War II: Barbarossa & the Eastern Front

front: Battle: turned the tide of the Eastern Front after Georgy Zhukov's Operation Uranus encircled the German 6th Army, capturing Field Marshal Friedrich Paulus
Battle: trapped German forces in a pocket dubbed "the Cauldron," where they were forced to eat their own horses
Battle: German invaders were stalled by the 1077th anti-aircraft regiment (mostly young women) while a local tractor factory was modified to produce T-34 tanks
Battle: Erich von Manstein's failed relief attempt, Operation Winter Storm, was followed by the final Soviet assault, Operation Ring
Battle: David Glantz's book Armageddon at Stalingrad debunks several myths about this battle
Event: Stalin fabricated this scheme, accusing predominantly Jewish physicians of conspiring to assassinate Soviet leaders
Ruler: attacked "rootless cosmopolitans" in a broader antisemitic campaign, labeling many Jews "cosmopolitan"
Ruler: forced Russian Jews toward the Jewish Autonomous Oblast around Birobidzhan in response to pogroms
Ruler: ordered the death of theater director Solomon Mikhoels
Event: Stalin's split with Yugoslav leader Josip Broz Tito opened this period, expelling Yugoslavia's Communist Party from Cominform via a 1948 resolution
Ruler: purged Romanian Communist Ana Pauker after she met with him
Ruler: orchestrated a 1929 coup in Tannu Tuva over its leader Donduk Kuular's association with Buddhist monks
Ruler: met with Jawaharlal Nehru, who could not stop discussing actor Prithviraj Kapoor's performance in the film Awaara
Ruler: Chinese People's Liberation Army founder Zhu De gave a speech defending him
Ruler: depicted as an eight-legged monster by the magazine Simplicissimus and the Catholic Library Service
work: Cult of Personality: Depictions in Visual Art

front: Ruler: painted standing behind a desk (twice) by Isaak Brodsky, and gesturing with a red pencil by Boris Vladimirsky
work: Cult of Personality: Depictions in Visual Art

front: Ruler: the largest depiction of him, a massive Prague statue by Otakar Svec, was destroyed in 1962
work: Cult of Personality: Depictions in Visual Art

front: Ruler: fifteen films portrayed him via actor Mikheil Gelovani, whose accurate Georgian accent Stalin disliked despite otherwise liking the films
work: Cult of Personality: Depictions in Visual Art

front: Work: this Armando Iannucci satirical film about Stalin's death features actors like Steve Buscemi using their own accents
work: Cult of Personality: Depictions in Visual Art

front: Work: this 1933 Osip Mandelstam poem describes Stalin's fingers as "ten thick worms" and "huge laughing cockroaches" on his top lip, leading to Mandelstam's arrest
Work: Stalin is fictionalized as "No. 1" in this Arthur Koestler novel, in which the character Rubashov is executed by his regime
Ruler: appears as a character in Vasily Grossman's novel Life and Fate, personally calling physicist Viktor Strum to reinstate him
Work: this Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn work documents Stalin's rule and the fate of a typical "zek" (prisoner)
Work: this Anna Akhmatova poem, circulated via samizdat, memorializes the victims of Stalin's purges, including her own son Lev's arrest
Work: this Anatoli Rybakov novel depicts Stalin's time in power
Ruler: wrote the pamphlet "Dialectical and Historical Materialism"
Ruler: satirized as a urinal statue ridden by statesmen in the novel The Yawning Heights
Work: this Yevgeny Yevtushenko poem about a 1941 massacre, read as an attack on Stalin's lingering antisemitism, was set to music by Shostakovich
Work: this Ismail Kadare novel centers on Stalin "rolling executions on his tongue like berries," referencing the Mandelstam affair
Ruler: praised in Pablo Neruda's poem collection Canto General
Ruler: implicitly compared to a yeti in Wislawa Szymborska's poem "Notes from a Nonexistent Himalayan Expedition"
Event: Nikita Khrushchev denounced Stalin in this 1956 speech, noting "not even the czars created prizes they named after themselves"
Event: this censorship-easing period followed Stalin's death and denunciation, allowing samizdat like Akhmatova's poems to circulate and renaming places like Mount Stalin and Stalingrad
Ruler: protesters beheaded a statue of him in one of the iconic images of the 1956 Hungarian Revolution
Ruler: a 1956 revolt in his native Georgia demanded that his memory be honored
Ruler: his regime has been historiographically compared to Nazi Germany's, notably in Hannah Arendt's The Origins of Totalitarianism and Timothy Snyder's Bloodlands
Figure: Stalin's daughter who defected to the United States and wrote the memoir Twenty Letters to a Friend
Figure: before defecting, she traveled to Sochi with her lover Kumwar Brajesh Singh, and later married the son-in-law of architect Frank Lloyd Wright
